does: RoadmapBench introduces a benchmark of 115 long-horizon coding tasks derived from real open-source version upgrades spanning 17 repositories and 5 programming languages. Each task requires a coding agent to implement functionality changes from a source version to a target version, with median modifications involving 3,700 lines of code across 51 files. The paper evaluates thirteen frontier models using this benchmark, finding that Claude-Opus-4.7 resolves only 39.1% of tasks while weaker models achieve 5.2%, establishing substantial performance gaps compared to single-issue bug-fix benchmarks. The evaluation methodology moves beyond coarse pass/fail metrics to capture success rates on genuinely complex, multi-file software engineering work that mirrors real-world development cycles spanning months of coordinated changes.
connects: This work extends prior benchmarking efforts in code generation and agent evaluation, which have predominantly focused on isolated bug fixes and single-file modifications in Python. RoadmapBench builds on datasets like HumanEval, SWE-Bench, and other code-centric benchmarks by scaling to long-horizon, multi-target development scenarios that require understanding architectural changes, dependency interactions, and coordinated modifications across large codebases. It responds to limitations in existing evaluation frameworks that use binary pass/fail outcomes and fail to capture the complexity of real software engineering workflows. The benchmark incorporates lessons from software engineering practice where version upgrades require months of development and cross-file coordination.
enables: RoadmapBench enables more realistic evaluation of coding agents deployed in production software development contexts, establishing baseline performance expectations for long-horizon tasks. The substantial performance gaps it reveals point toward future research in improving agent planning, multi-file coordination, and architectural understanding in code generation. The benchmark supports development of better prompting strategies, tool use patterns, and agent architectures for sustained multi-target development. It provides a foundation for measuring progress on genuinely challenging software engineering problems beyond isolated bug fixes, facilitating research into how agents can better understand version migration requirements, manage inter-file dependencies, and execute coordinated changes across large codebases.
